titleAr: لو كنت من هاشم ، أو من بني أسد ،
titleEn: If You Were from Hashim or Banu Asad

verses.sadr: لو كنت من هاشم ، أو من بني أسد ،
verses.ajuz: أو عبد شمس ، أو أصحاب اللوا الصيد

verses.sadr: أو من بني نوفل ، أو رهط مطلب
أو في الذؤابة من قوم ذوي حسب ،
verses.ajuz: لم تصبح اليوم نكسا ثاني الجيد

verses.sadr: أو من بني زهرة الأخيار قد علموا ،
verses.ajuz: أو من بني جمح البيض المناجيد

verses.sadr: أو في لذؤابة من تيم ، رضيت بهم ،
verses.ajuz: أو من بني خلف الخضر الجلاعيد

verses.sadr: يا آل تيم ألا ينهى سفيهكم ،

verses.ajuz: وطلحة بن عبيد الله ذو الجود

translationEn: If you were from Hashim, or from Banu Asad, or Abd Shams, or the lion-hearted bearers of the standard — or from Banu Nawfal, or the clan of Muttalib — by God, you would not have dared to threaten me. Or if you belonged to the elite of a people of noble lineage, you would not have become today a wretch with a bowed neck. Or from Banu Zuhra, the finest — they know — or from Banu Jumah, the fair-faced and illustrious, or from the cream of Taym, whom I would accept, or from Banu Khalaf, the verdant and mighty. O clan of Taym, will you not restrain your fool before the hurling of words like boulders? Were it not for the Messenger — for I shall never disobey him until my grave conceals me — and the Companion of the Cave, whom I shall always honor, and Talha ibn Ubaydullah, the generous one — you have been struck with a scandalous disgrace that leaves even the sound among the people as if dead. But I shall redirect my efforts and divert my words away from you with firm speech, not mere threats, toward al-Zibari, for baseness has sworn him allegiance, or toward the vilest of the sons of Abud.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the forelock, the elite or cream of a people
niche in a grave, burial place

context: A poem of satirical rebuke in which Hassan ibn Thabit enumerates the noble clans of Quraysh, implying that his target belongs to none of them. He restrains himself out of obedience to the Prophet Muhammad, respect for Abu Bakr (the Companion of the Cave), and Talha ibn Ubaydullah, redirecting his satire toward Ibn al-Zibari and the enemies of Islam.

titleEn: Has the Eye Not Known Its Sleeplessness?

verses.ajuz: ن : عاد له الشر من عادها

translationEn: Has the eye not known its sleeplessness, and the flowing of tears, and their exhaustion? Remembering the disheveled one after slumber, and the place where tent-ropes and pegs once lay — when the rumbling spring clouds pass over her courtyard, they pour their rain upon it. She rose to show you her flowing-haired beauty, bending under the weight that burdened her. Her face like the face of a spring gazelle traversing hillocks and slopes. The night drove him toward the thorny trees, fearing overcast skies and their biting cold. If I should die, do not marry one who forsakes his clan, one of their enviers, who considers praising their honor an insult out of folly, and who hates those who lead them. And if they reproach him once, their provisions are consumed in his night-plotting. One like me could be endured, yet I burden myself with what weighs me down. I shall give the clan what they sought from me and prove their threats false. I shall bear the burden if a debt afflicts them, and strike with the sword whoever plots against them. Yathrib knows that we are lions within it, shaking off their cloaks for battle, thrusting spears into the chests of warriors until we break their shafts — when they are roused and the forbearing become youthful in passion, and the people gather their hosts. The chaste women say to the righteous: evil returns upon whoever opposes her. We made bounty a shield against hardship, and in times of strain, we were their pillars.
context: A poem combining the nasib (amatory prelude) with tribal boasting (fakhr). Hassan recalls a beloved and a ruined campsite, then shifts to declaring his loyalty to his clan and their martial prowess in Yathrib (Medina). The poem showcases the Ansar's pride in their role as defenders, a recurring theme in Hassan's pre-Islamic and Islamic poetry.

titleEn: If You Reform, You Are Still an Abidi
translationEn: If you reform, you are still an Abidi, and the reform of the Abidi leads only to corruption. If you turn corrupt, then I have found you — as I well know — far from all rectitude. You find him just as he has always been, with his blunders and the foolishness of his heart. Error is plain in him, never beyond his reach, yet he is helpless before the paths of right guidance. Besides all this, a wretch stood up to insult me, like a pig wallowing in ashes. I bear witness that your mother was a harlot, and that your father is among the worst of mankind. I shall never cease satirizing the Abidi, as long as time endures and the caller calls. These verses have traveled and endure — the transmitters recite them in every valley. Cursed be Abid and his kinsmen, for their final destination is the worst of returns.
context: A fierce satirical poem directed at a man from the Abid clan. Hassan declares that neither reform nor corruption can change his target's fundamentally base nature. The poem exemplifies the hija tradition in which the poet's verses become a lasting weapon, transmitted by reciters across the land as an enduring mark of shame.

titleEn: Mongrels When Their Lineage Is Traced
translationEn: Mongrels when their lineage is traced — slaves, ruffians, with fire-sticks that yield no spark.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: mongrels, people of mixed or dishonorable descent

context: A single-verse satirical fragment that delivers a devastating tribal insult. Hassan packs three layers of abuse into one line: impure lineage (mongrels), servile status (slaves and ruffians), and incompetence (fire-sticks that fail to produce flame, metaphorically indicating cowardice and inability).

titleEn: We Are Not Drinkers Beneath a Cloak's Shade
translationEn: We are not drinkers beneath the shade of a cloak, counting out a he-goat and a lancet for the tavern. Rather, we are noble drinkers who, when they become merry, lavish the pure milk and the layered hump-fat. You would think them dead in the stillness of Halima, but if you come to them, you will praise their hospitality the next day. If you visit them, you find around their dwellings crushed musk and saffron scattered about. You see upon the folds of their carpets, spread out, sandals and fine goblets, and striped garments — and a dew-drenched youth hurrying along, his cheek pressed against brocade whose borders have been finely cut.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hump-fat of a camel, a delicacy

context: A poem of tribal pride (fakhr) in which Hassan contrasts the miserly drinkers who count their pennies at a tavern with his own people, who are generous hosts of legendary hospitality. The vivid descriptions of musk, saffron, fine carpets, and brocade paint a picture of aristocratic Ansari life in Yathrib.

titleEn: By God, I Do Not Know — Though I Ask

verses.ajuz: مهانة ، ذات الخيف ، ألأم ، أم سعد

verses.sadr: وكان أبو سرح عقيما فلم يكن
verses.ajuz: له ولد حتى دعيت له بعد

translationEn: By God, I do not know — though I inquire: is Muhana, she of the tent at al-Khayf, more base, or is it Saad? A mongrel slave, bright red of complexion, with taut sinews at the nape and tightly curled hair. Abu Sarh was barren and had no offspring until you were claimed as his son after the fact.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: mongrel, of mixed or base descent
sinew, tendon at the back of the neck

context: A short satirical poem attacking an opponent's lineage by questioning his parentage and physical features. Hassan suggests the target was not a legitimate son but was adopted or falsely attributed to Abu Sarh. The rhetorical question comparing two figures in baseness is a classic hija device.

titleEn: The Merciful Has Cursed a Host Led Astray

verses.ajuz: يبين فيه اللؤم من كان يهتدي

translationEn: The Merciful has cursed a host led by the pretender of Banu Shuja to war against Muhammad — an ill-omened, accursed wretch who was despised from of old, in whom baseness is plain to anyone with guidance. He led them into error until they crumbled, for his command was one of misguidance, not right direction. Then my Lord sent down His armies for the Prophet and supported him with victory in every battle. And verily the reward of God for every believer in His oneness is gardens of Paradise in which they shall dwell forever.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Merciful, one of God's names
Paradise, the highest garden in heaven
monotheist, one who professes God's oneness

context: An Islamic poem praising the Prophet Muhammad and condemning those who waged war against him. Hassan attributes the enemy's defeat to divine intervention, with God sending down His armies in support of the Prophet. The poem ends with a declaration of the reward awaiting believers in Paradise, reflecting Hassan's role as the poet-defender of early Islam.

titleEn: The Vile Son of Nabigha Claimed
translationEn: The vile son of Nabigha claimed that we would not place our honor beneath Muhammad's cause. Our wealth and our lives are shields for him — whoever does good shall be rewarded and praised. Truthful young men, like lions, fierce in battle — whoever meets them on the day of combat shall flee. The people of Ibn Nabigha are base and humiliated, unable to face even the whistle of the thunderer. Your father built them a house of shortcoming in disbelief and baseness — what a wretched house of origin!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to do, to perform (a good deed)
fierce in battle, kindlers of war

context: A poem defending the Ansar's dedication to the Prophet Muhammad against the taunts of Ibn Nabigha, who mocked their willingness to sacrifice for Islam. Hassan affirms that their wealth and lives are shields for Muhammad, praises the Ansar as fearless warriors, and returns the insult by condemning the enemy's lineage as one of disbelief and baseness.

titleEn: I Asked All of Quraysh — Their Worst Are Banu Abid
translationEn: I asked all of Quraysh, and their worst are Banu Abid — disfigured of face is the Abidi. When they sit in the middle of the assembly, they call out to each other like the croaking frogs of spring swarming about. Sayfi was never one to honor a pledge — the back of a fox, confounded at some of the watering-places.
context: A satirical attack on Banu Abid, whom Hassan declares the worst clan of Quraysh. The comparison of their speech in assembly to croaking frogs is a classic degrading image. The final verse attacks a man named Sayfi as untrustworthy, likened to the back of a fox that cannot find its way to water — a metaphor for cowardice and incompetence.

titleEn: If You Seek the Mightiest Chief

verses.sadr: ليس يرى من ضرب كبش بدا

translationEn: If you seek the mightiest chief among men, then go to Saad — Saad ibn Zayd. Take him as your army, for he is no coward who trembles and shakes. He sees no shame in striking the leading ram.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ram; metaphor for a leader in battle

context: A short rajaz poem praising Saad ibn Zayd, an Ansari chief known for his bravery. The rajaz meter, with its energetic rhythm, was often used for war chants and brief tributes. Hassan recommends Saad as a warrior who is both fearless and decisive, using the image of striking the leading ram to signify his willingness to confront enemy leaders head-on.

titleEn: I Am the Son of Khalda and al-Agharr
translationEn: I am the son of Khalda, and al-Agharr, and the two Maliks, and their helper — and the nobles of your people, if you send to the people of Yathrib one who inquires. I strove through the mansions of the outer quarters and the inner ones, exerting myself. You shall come to know, and you — when your knowledge is certain — shall praise them. They are the feeders when the years of drought settle and become still. The tops of the cooking vessels in the bowls of the poplar, frozen solid with fat.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Yathrib, the pre-Islamic name for Medina

context: A poem of tribal self-praise (fakhr) in which Hassan enumerates his ancestors and the noble lineages of his Khazraj clan. The poem celebrates the generosity of his people who feed others even in times of drought, a paramount virtue in Arabian society. The image of frozen fat on cooking pots indicates abundant food prepared even in the harshest conditions.

titleAr: فمن يك منهم ذا خلاق ، فإنه
titleEn: Whoever Among Them Has Good Character

verses.sadr: فمن يك منهم ذا خلاق ، فإنه

translationEn: Whoever among them possesses good character, his character shall restrain him from the injustice that has been confirmed against him.
context: A single-verse fragment functioning as a moral aphorism within a longer satirical context. Hassan suggests that only those among his opponents who possess genuine moral fiber will be restrained from committing further injustice. The implication is that most of them lack such character entirely.

titleAr: لعمرك ما تنفك عن طلب الخنا
titleEn: By Your Life, They Never Cease Seeking Disgrace

verses.sadr: لعمرك ما تنفك عن طلب الخنا
verses.ajuz: بنو زهرة الأنذال ما عاش واحد
إذا حضرت يوما من الدهر ماجد

translationEn: By your life, Banu Zuhra, those low-born wretches, never cease seeking disgrace as long as one of them lives. Their endeavors are base, their ancestors fall short of goodness toward the foreign neighbor — they muster only against him. Not one among them, when noble deeds and high honor present themselves on any day of time, proves to be glorious.
context: A satirical attack on Banu Zuhra, a clan of Quraysh whom Hassan denounces as perpetually ignoble. The three verses build a systematic indictment: they seek disgrace, their efforts are base and hostile toward strangers, and when the occasion calls for honor, not a single one of them rises to it.

titleAr: لقد كان قيس في اللئام مرددا ،
titleEn: Qays Was Tossed About Among the Base

verses.sadr: لقد كان قيس في اللئام مرددا ،

translationEn: Qays was tossed about among the base — the residue of a chick, the mine of baseness, a toiler. A wretched birth from Sumayya — she is a wretched Umayya whose glory is the worst of inherited possessions. In open fornication, publicly, from the most foolish among them — she surpassed them all in every arena. She brought forth Qays, the basest of people in origin, whenever the base origins of men are mentioned.
context: A savage genealogical attack on a man named Qays, whose mother Sumayya is slandered as having conceived him in fornication. Hassan traces his baseness through both parents, making him 'the mine of baseness.' This type of genealogical satire was particularly devastating in Arab tribal society where lineage determined social standing.

titleAr: وما طلعت شمس النهار ولا بدت
titleEn: No Sun Has Risen Nor Appeared Upon You with Glory

verses.sadr: وما طلعت شمس النهار ولا بدت
verses.ajuz: عليك ، بمجد ، يا ابن مقطوعة اليد
تبنى عليك اللؤم في كل مشهد

verses.sadr: إذا الدهر عف في تقادم عهده ،
verses.ajuz: على عار قوم ، كان لؤمك في غد

translationEn: No sun of day has risen nor appeared upon you with any glory, O son of the woman whose hand was cut off. Your father was a foundling, the basest of men in station — baseness is built upon you in every gathering. When time, in the passage of its ages, erases the shame of other peoples, your baseness shall remain fresh as tomorrow.
context: A fierce satirical poem in which Hassan denies his target any claim to honor. The reference to 'the woman whose hand was cut off' is a specific genealogical insult. The final verse is particularly devastating: while time erases other peoples' shame, this man's baseness remains eternally fresh.

titleEn: Whose Is the Child at the Edge of the Valley?

verses.ajuz: في الترب ملقى ، غير ذي مهد

translationEn: Whose is the child at the edge of the valley floor, cast in the dust, without a cradle? A fair-skinned, gentle woman of Abd Shams bore him — smooth of cheek. She rushes toward the crying, wailing — O Hind, you are hard in your anger! When she wishes, she calls for a censer to be lit for her with Indian aloe-wood. The resemblance of the boy was overcome, and the blackness of kinky, jet-black hair appeared. She gestured to the base woman, whose habit was to crack the knuckle-bones with a tough molar.
context: A poem that combines narrative and satire, telling the story of an abandoned child in the valley of Mecca. Hassan uses the imagery of an infant cast in the dust without a cradle to question the legitimacy of an enemy's birth. The details about Hind and the women of Abd Shams root this poem in the rivalries of early Islam.

titleEn: Whose Are These Abandoned Infants?

verses.sadr: فيهم صبي له أم لها نسب ،
verses.ajuz: في ذروة من ذرى الأحساب ، أياد

verses.sadr: تقول وهنا ، وقد جد المخاض بها :
verses.ajuz: يا ليتني كنت أرعى الشول للغادي

translationEn: Whose are these abandoned infants, cast away, squirming through the night in the sandy plain of Ajyad? She labored through the night — her midwives were none but the wild beasts and the jinn of the valley. Among them is a child whose mother has a lineage at the peak of noble pedigrees, from Iyad. She says weakly, as labor grips her: 'Would that I were herding the she-camels for the traveler!' They left him, noble of face, covered in dust, though her uncle and father are the lords of the assembly.
context: A companion poem to the previous one, continuing the theme of abandoned infants in Mecca's valleys. Hassan paints a vivid and pathetic scene of a woman of noble lineage giving birth alone in the wilderness with only wild beasts and jinn as attendants. The irony that her family are lords of the assembly while she is abandoned heightens the satirical force.

titleAr: لقد علم الأقوام أن ابن هاشم
titleEn: The People Know That the Son of Hashim

verses.sadr: لقد علم الأقوام أن ابن هاشم
verses.ajuz: هو الغصن ذو الأفنان لا الواحد الوغد
فدونك فالصق مثل ما لصق القرد

verses.sadr: وإن سنام المجد من آل هاشم

verses.ajuz: ولكن هجين ليس يورى له زند

verses.sadr: وأنت زنيم نيط في آل هاشم ،
verses.ajuz: كما نيط خلف الراكب القدح الفرد

translationEn: The people know that the son of Hashim is the branch with many boughs, not the solitary wretch. You have no recognized origin among them, so go ahead and cling to them the way a monkey clings. The summit of glory among the family of Hashim belongs to the sons of the daughter of Makhzum, while your father is a slave. The mixed clans of Zuhra never produced a generous man from among you, nor did glory ever come near your old women. You are not like al-Abbas, nor like his maternal cousin — rather, you are a mongrel whose fire-stick yields no spark. You are an appendage attached to the family of Hashim, as a single arrow is hung behind the rider. A man whose mother was Sumayya, and the dark-skinned one — overcome when hardship reaches its peak.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: appendage, one attached to a tribe not his own
fire-stick, the stick rubbed to produce fire

context: A poem praising the lineage of Banu Hashim — the Prophet Muhammad's clan — while attacking an opponent who falsely claims connection to them. Hassan uses vivid metaphors: the opponent clings like a monkey, is hung on like a spare arrow, and his fire-stick yields no spark. The poem contrasts genuine Hashemite nobility with the pretender's servile origins.

titleEn: May God Have Mercy on Nafi ibn Budayl
translationEn: May God have mercy on Nafi ibn Budayl — the mercy sought by one who yearns for the reward of holy struggle. He was patient, truthful in speech — when the people spoke at length, he spoke the word of rectitude. I was, before meeting him, ignorant of his worth, but now his loss has struck my heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: holy struggle, striving in God's path

context: An elegy (ritha) for Nafi ibn Budayl, a companion who fell in battle. Hassan praises his patience, truthfulness, and wisdom, confessing that he only came to truly appreciate the man after his death. The poem reflects the deep bonds formed among the early Muslim community through shared struggle.

titleAr: غدا أهل حضني ذي المجاز بسحرة
titleEn: The People of the Slopes of Dhu al-Majaz Set Out at Dawn

verses.sadr: غدا أهل حضني ذي المجاز بسحرة
verses.ajuz: وجار ابن حرب بالمحصب ما يغدو
وأصبحت رخوا ما تخب وما تعدو

translationEn: The people of the slopes of Dhu al-Majaz set out at dawn, while the dependent of Ibn Harb at al-Muhassab does not stir. Hisham ibn al-Walid clothed you with his garments — wear them out and replace them with new ones afterward. He fulfilled his desire from him and departed in the morning, while you became slack, neither trotting nor galloping. If the elders who witnessed Badr had been present, the backs of horses would have been drenched with freshly drawn rose-red blood. The flatulent donkey's reins did not protect it, and Hind did not prevent the disgrace of her father.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to replace, to get a substitute
Badr, site of the first major Islamic battle

context: A satirical poem mocking an enemy associated with the Quraysh leadership. Hassan references the Battle of Badr, suggesting that had the elders of Quraysh who died there been alive, there would have been real fighting. The mention of Hind (bint Utba, wife of Abu Sufyan) connects this to the rivalry between the Muslims and the Meccan aristocracy.

titleAr: شق له من اسمه كي يجله ،
titleEn: He Derived for Him from His Name to Honor Him

verses.sadr: شق له من اسمه كي يجله ،

verses.ajuz: من الرسل والأوثان في الأرض تعبد
بذلك ما عمرت في الناس أشهد

verses.sadr: تعاليت رب الناس عن قول من دعا

translationEn: He derived for him from His own name to honor him: the Lord of the Throne is Mahmud (Praised), and this one is Muhammad (Praised One). A prophet who came to us after despair and an interval between messengers, while idols were worshipped upon the earth. He became a shining lamp and a guide, gleaming as a polished Indian sword gleams. He warned us of the Fire and gave glad tidings of Paradise, and taught us Islam — so God we praise. You are the God of truth, my Lord and Creator — to that I bear witness as long as I live among people. Exalted are You, Lord of mankind, above the words of those who call upon any god besides You — You are the Most High and Most Glorious. Yours is all creation and all bounty and all command — from You we seek guidance, and You alone we worship. For the reward of God for every believer in His oneness is gardens of Paradise in which he shall dwell forever.
context: One of Hassan's most celebrated Islamic poems, praising the Prophet Muhammad by connecting his name to God's attribute al-Mahmud (the Praised). The poem describes Muhammad as a light that came after a long interval without prophets, then transitions into a declaration of pure monotheism echoing Quranic language. The verse 'from You we seek guidance and You alone we worship' recalls Surat al-Fatiha.

titleEn: Tell the Destitute That Goodness Has Left Them

verses.sadr: من ذا الذي عنده رحلي ، وراحلتي ،
verses.ajuz: ورزق أهلي ، إذا لم يؤنسوا المطرا

verses.sadr: أم من نعاتب لا نخشى جنادعه ،
verses.ajuz: إذا اللسان عتا في القول ، أو عثرا

verses.sadr: لم يترك الله منا بعده أحدا ،
verses.ajuz: ولم يعش بعده أنثى ، ولا ذكرا
وكان أمرا من امر الله قد قدرا

translationEn: Tell the destitute that goodness has left them — it departed with the Prophet from them at dawn. Who now holds my saddle and my riding-camel, and the provision of my family when they see no rain? Or whom can we reproach without fearing his retort, when the tongue grows bold in speech or stumbles? He was the light, he was the radiance we followed — after God — and he was our hearing and our sight. Would that on the day they laid him in his grave-niche, concealed him, and cast the earth-clods over him, God had not left a single one of us after him, and no woman or man had lived beyond him. The necks of all Banu al-Najjar were bowed in humility, for it was a matter decreed by God.
context: One of Hassan's most moving elegies, composed upon the death of the Prophet Muhammad in 632 CE. The poem expresses the devastating sense of loss felt by the Muslim community. Hassan declares that the Prophet was their light, their hearing and sight after God, and wishes that no one had survived him. The bowing of Banu al-Najjar's necks — Hassan's own clan — reflects the collective grief of the Ansar.

titleEn: You Were the Pupil of My Eye
translationEn: You were the pupil of my eye, and now my eye has gone blind over you. Let whoever wishes die after you, for it was only your death that I feared.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: eye, the seeing part of the eye
to fear, to be on guard against

context: A brief but devastating elegy, likely for the Prophet Muhammad, though it has also been attributed to laments for other beloved figures. The metaphor of the departed being 'the pupil of my eye' — and the eye going blind upon their loss — is one of the most quoted couplets in Arabic elegy. The second verse's stark declaration that all other deaths are now insignificant captures the totality of grief.

titleEn: Beware — I Have Grown Old and Trials Overcame Me
translationEn: Beware of me — I have grown old and calamities overcame me in my concern for you as old age whitened my hair. You made me a target for the base — every one of them shoots his baseness at me, whether reaching the mark or falling short. Until their gums bleed and a black stain grows upon them, its roots like the bitter colocynth plant. Did you serve them my honor, mocking me heedlessly? May your mother be bereaved — give them to devour something other than my honor! A target at which the archers take turns, as though they were shooting at a boulder on the slope of al-Mashar.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to give as slaughter, to give over
al-Mashar, a sacred site near Muzdalifa

context: A poem of reproach and self-defense from Hassan's old age. He addresses someone who exposed him to the attacks of base men, making his honor a target for all. Despite his advancing years, Hassan's poetic venom remains potent, and he demands that his honor be spared while threatening his attackers with satirical retaliation.

titleEn: Al-Nadira, the Lady of the Curtained Chamber

verses.ajuz: ما رد طرف العين ذو شفر
من غير ما نسب ولا صهر

verses.sadr: لو كنت لا تهوين لم تردي ،
verses.ajuz: أو كنت ، ما تلوين في وكر

translationEn: Al-Nadira, the lady of the curtained chamber, traveled to you by night, though she was not wont to travel. I stopped in the desert to ask her: how did you find the way to the traveler's camp? The white camels had loosened their halters from the weakness they showed, and their flaws had overtaken their beauty from the emaciation that afflicted them. We used to, when the midday heat settled upon us, steal through it on swift she-camels with crooked necks — winding, bleating, carrying us aloft, excused from the hard driving and the shouting. Facing every blazing noon, they breathe in rings of brass. And her resting-place at every halt was like the roosting-spot of the dark, dusty sandgrouse. A chameleon rose upon a branch and confronted us, or was about to swish its tail. And bearing the long day when its grasshoppers chirred from the heat of noon. And the dark night I travel through with the company across the barren, desolate waste, where the echo mourns its brother as the bereaved mourns the companion of the grave. Its darkness forms a barrier until it becomes oppressive to the night-traveler. I have shown the riders their families and guided them through dusty, trackless wastes. I offered my riding-mount and was generous with it to them in hardship and in ease. Misfortunes do not crush me, nor does my chest grow narrow with any need. My least word exhausts whoever tries to match me — by your life, I am no idle talker. I honor whoever honors me, and my claw falls upon the one who hides enmity. I do not steal what other poets have uttered — rather their poetry does not match mine. My lineage forbids that to me, and a speech like the cutting-edges of rock. And my brother from among the perceptive jinn, who weaves words in the finest ink. O Nadira, what is between you and me is rupture, and the estrangement you have caused. Be generous, for generosity is a virtue, and requite the sword with some of what it cuts. I swore I would never forget you, as long as the eyelid returns the eye's glance. I swore I would never forget your conversation, so long as the dissolute one remembers the pleasure of wine. You are more beautiful, when you emerged for us on the day of departure in the courtyard of the palace, than a pearl for which kings pay dearly — one nurtured by the swirling depths of the sea. With rounded calves, resembling two papyrus reeds in a deep, overflowing pool. She grows as her noble stock grows, in the abode of the people of glory and pride. Longing overtakes me and I remember her, without any tie of kinship or marriage — like the memory of a thirsty man who has no water on the summit of a steep, rugged peak. She would sit beside me, but shyness and the constraint of modesty prevented me. If you did not love, you would not have come; or if you were, you would not be sheltering in a nest. I would surely have come to him seeking him — so preserve your modesty, and accept my excuse. Tell al-Nadira, if you encounter her: the generous one is not the companion of scarcity. My people are Banu al-Najjar — their support is gracious, and they are ever present to aid me. Death stands before me — I am not oppressed — and the people of noble deeds from Banu Amr, a deep-rooted stronghold whose fortresses of honor have been ours since ancient times.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: echo (believed to be a bird in pre-Islamic lore)

context: One of Hassan's longest and most ambitious poems, combining the traditional pre-Islamic qasida structure with personal lyric and tribal pride. It opens with the mysterious nocturnal visit of al-Nadira, transitions through a desert journey passage (rahil) with vivid descriptions of camels and landscape, moves into poetic self-assertion and boasts of originality, and concludes with a love passage and a declaration of tribal strength. The mention of a jinn companion who aids his poetry reflects the pre-Islamic belief that great poets were inspired by spirits.

titleEn: A Harsh Night in Yathrib Revisited Me

verses.ajuz: وهم ، إذا ما نوم الناس ، مسهر
وكم من كريم يبتلى ، ثم يصبر

verses.sadr: أغر كلون البدر من آل هاشم ،
وكنا نرى في جعفر من محمد
فما زال في الإسلام من آل هاشم
بهم تكشف اللأواء في كل مأزق
verses.ajuz: عماس ، إذا ما ضاق بالقوم مصدر
عقيل ، وماء العود من حيث يعصر

translationEn: A harsh night in Yathrib revisited me — a sorrow that keeps me sleepless when others sleep. The memory of a beloved stirred a flowing tear, and the causes of weeping are remembrance. What a trial — the loss of a beloved is an affliction, yet many a noble man is tested and then endures. I saw the best of the believers go one after another to their fates, while I was left among those held back. May God not distance the slain who fell in succession at Mu'tah — among them Ja'far of the Two Wings, and Zayd, and Abdullah — when they fell together while the causes of death hovered about them. On the morning they went forth leading the believers, led to death by one of auspicious nature, radiant — brilliant as the color of the full moon, from the family of Hashim, refusing when asked to submit to injustice, bold. He fought with the lance until he died unpropped by any pillow, in a battle-ground where spears shattered. His reward became that of the martyrs — gardens and dense, green orchards. We used to see in Ja'far something of Muhammad — loyalty, and firm command when he commanded. There has never ceased to be among the family of Hashim pillars of honor unassailable and cause for pride. They are the mountain of Islam, and the people around them are rubble beside a peak that awes and overwhelms. Through them affliction is lifted in every dire strait when the way out narrows for the people. They are God's allies, upon whom He sent down His judgment, and among them is this purified Book. Noble lords among them are Ja'far and his maternal brother Ali, and among them is Ahmad, the Chosen One, and Hamza, and al-Abbas, and among them Aqil — the essence of the wood is from where it is pressed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to revisit, to come upon at night
the one with two wings (title of Ja'far)
Mu'tah, site of a battle in 629 CE

context: A major elegy commemorating the Battle of Mu'tah (629 CE), where three Muslim commanders fell in succession: Zayd ibn Haritha, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib (the Prophet's cousin, known as 'the one with two wings'), and Abdullah ibn Rawaha. Hassan mourns them while praising the entire Hashemite family — including Ali, Hamza, and al-Abbas — as the pillars of Islam. The poem is both a personal lament and a political statement about the centrality of the Prophet's family.

titleEn: I Was Told That Abu Mundhir
translationEn: I was told that Abu Mundhir rivals you for the title of al-Harith the Younger. The back of your neck is more handsome than his face, and your mother is better than al-Mundhir himself. Your left hand in its difficulty is like his right hand upon the impoverished. How vast is the distance between you in generosity, in valor, in goodness, and in appearance!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the back of the neck, the nape

context: A poem praising al-Harith the Younger by mocking Abu Mundhir's pretension to rival him. Hassan employs a series of devastating comparisons: the back of al-Harith's neck surpasses Abu Mundhir's face, and al-Harith's left hand gives more generously than Abu Mundhir's right. The poem is both panegyric and satire, praising one by diminishing the other.

titleEn: O Eye, Pour Forth Your Scanty Tears

verses.sadr: قد أتانا من قتلهم ما كفانا ،

translationEn: O eye, pour forth your scanty tears, and remember in times of ease the people of the graves. Remember Mu'tah and what befell there, on the day they turned away in the onslaught of the raid — when they departed and left behind Zayd, who was the finest refuge for the destitute and the captive. The beloved of the best of all creation altogether — the master of mankind, whose love resides in the hearts. That is Ahmad, beside whom there is none other — in him is both my grief and my joy. Then weep for the Khazraji with your tears — a chief who was there, no rare visitor. Enough news of their killing has reached us, so we spend the night in sorrow, not in joy.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Mu'tah, site of a major battle

context: Another elegy for the fallen at the Battle of Mu'tah, focusing on Zayd ibn Haritha, the Prophet's adopted son and the first of the three commanders to fall. Hassan calls on the eye to weep and remember, linking the martyrs to the Prophet's love. The mention of a Khazraji chief connects the poem to Hassan's own Ansari community. The final verse's stark admission of overwhelming grief reflects the magnitude of the losses.

titleAr: أوفت بنو عمرو بن عوف نذرها ،
titleEn: Banu Amr ibn Awf Fulfilled Their Vow

verses.sadr: أوفت بنو عمرو بن عوف نذرها ،
إن لم تروا مددا له وكتيبة
فعدمت ما ولد ابن عمرو منذر
أبلغ بني بكر ، إذا ما جئتهم ،
من خير خندف كلها ، بعد الذي
حاشا بني عمرو بن عوف إنهم

translationEn: Banu Amr ibn Awf fulfilled their vow, while Banu al-Najjar were stained with treachery. They failed on the day of zealous duty — they are not, in that regard, among the righteous. They forgot Muhammad's testament concerning his son-in-law and exchanged honor for an abode of ruin. Did you leave him alone in a place of loss, visited by the rabble of the cities? Heartsick, calling upon his absent supporters — woe to you, O company of the Ansar! Would that you had honored your covenants then, and ransomed him with your hearing and sight! His neighbors, the groaners around his houses, betrayed him — by the Lord of the House of curtains! If you do not send him reinforcements and a battalion leading the vanguard of a mighty host — then I renounce whatever al-Mundhir son of Amr has fathered, until they make their forces halt at Sirar. By God, they will never keep faith after their leader, even if they swore by the saddle-cloth of a donkey. Convey to Banu Bakr, when you reach them, blame — what wretched places for marriage alliances! They betrayed a man white as the crescent moon, blameless, whose sword-blades were tempered with a flint of fire. From the best of all Khindif, after him whom God aided to triumph over the disbelievers. You obeyed the enemy concerning him, though you could have been, had you wished, in a place apart and safe. Let the rumor-mongers not suppose that the blood of the people of the house will go unavenged. Excepted are Banu Amr ibn Awf — their resting-places are written among the righteous.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Khindif, a major Arab tribal confederation

context: A politically charged poem addressing the crisis of succession after the Prophet's death. Hassan reproaches the Ansar for failing to uphold the Prophet's testament regarding his son-in-law (Ali ibn Abi Talib), praising Banu Amr ibn Awf for their loyalty while condemning Banu al-Najjar — his own clan — for their betrayal. This remarkable poem shows Hassan turning his satirical powers against his own people for what he sees as a failure of duty, making it one of the most significant political poems of early Islam.

titleAr: وأفلت يوم الروع أوس بن خالد ،
titleEn: Aws ibn Khalid Escaped on the Day of Terror

verses.sadr: وأفلت يوم الروع أوس بن خالد ،

translationEn: Aws ibn Khalid escaped on the day of terror, spewing blood like a nosebleed, his chest dyed crimson.
context: A single-verse satirical fragment mocking Aws ibn Khalid for fleeing from battle. The vivid image of him spewing blood with his chest dyed crimson suggests he was wounded but fled rather than standing and fighting. In Arabian martial culture, fleeing from battle was the ultimate disgrace, and Hassan immortalizes this shame in a single devastating line.
